skill.name: when-automating-github-actions-use-workflow-automation
skill.description: Guides GitHub Actions workflow creation, optimization, security hardening, matrix testing, and debugging through coordinated AI agent roles.
skill.summary: Create, optimize, secure, and debug GitHub Actions CI/CD workflows.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: Static findings are mostly documentation examples for a GitHub Actions automation skill, so many weak-crypto and broad command matches are false positives. Several instructions still create real operational risk: remote alpha package execution, verbose local replay with a secrets file, and debug logging that prints environment variables. No evidence found of prompt injection or confirmed malicious exfiltration intent, so this is high risk rather than blocked.

high_findings.title: Potential Secret Exposure Through Debug Logging
high_findings.description: The debug workflow example prints sorted environment variables during a GitHub Actions run. This can expose tokens, deployment values, or sensitive configuration in logs, especially when copied into real workflows.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment dump is explicit, and GitHub Actions logs commonly persist debug output. The finding is high confidence even though the surrounding text presents it as temporary debugging.

high_findings.title: Verbose Local Replay Reads Secret File
high_findings.description: The local reproduction example runs act with a .secrets file and verbose output. This is legitimate debugging, but it increases the chance that local credentials are exposed in terminal logs or copied into issue reports.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command explicitly consumes a secrets file and enables verbose mode. The risk depends on user behavior, so confidence is high but not absolute.

high_findings.title: Remote Alpha Package Execution
high_findings.description: The skill instructs users to execute an alpha npm package through npx. That can download and run third-party code at use time, creating supply-chain risk if the package or resolution changes.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The npx invocation is direct and uses an alpha package channel. It appears related to the skill purpose, but it still executes remote code.

medium_findings.title: Repository-Mutating Shell Workflows
medium_findings.description: The skill provides commands that create branches, commit workflow files, push to origin, create pull requests, and push fixes to main. These are expected for CI/CD automation but can change repositories if run without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are concrete and repository-mutating. They are legitimate for the skill domain, which lowers severity from high.

medium_findings.title: GitHub Token Usage Requires Least Privilege
medium_findings.description: The skill references GITHUB_TOKEN permissions and authenticated GitHub API usage. The guidance recommends least privilege, but generated workflows must still avoid overly broad token scopes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Token handling is clearly present, but the skill discusses safe configuration rather than credential theft. The risk is conditional on generated workflow quality.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static weak-crypto matches appear to be caused by references to pinning GitHub Actions by commit SHA and security workflow terminology. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, or cryptographic implementation code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected context uses SHA as an action-pinning concept, not as a weak hash algorithm. No cryptographic code was present in the analyzed files.

low_findings.title: Home Directory Cache Paths Are Expected CI Paths
low_findings.description: Static hidden-file findings for home-directory paths are cache examples for npm and Cargo in GitHub Actions. These paths do not show arbitrary hidden file collection or exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The paths are inside an actions/cache example and refer to package manager caches. This is a common benign CI pattern.

low_findings.title: Network Finding Is Documentation-Level GitHub API Guidance
low_findings.description: The network finding points to guidance about GitHub API rate limiting and using GITHUB_TOKEN. No evidence found of a hardcoded external endpoint or credential exfiltration request.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Only explanatory text was found at the flagged line. It still matters because generated workflows may call GitHub APIs, but the static signal itself is weak.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment Dump in CI Debug Step
dangerous_patterns.description: A workflow debug step runs env output in CI logs. Marketplace users should remove this pattern or redact sensitive names before publishing workflows.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command pattern is explicit and risky in CI systems where logs are retained.

dangerous_patterns.title: Secret File Passed to Verbose act Run
dangerous_patterns.description: A local act command uses a .secrets file with verbose logging. This can leak local secret values through logs or screenshots.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The static filesystem finding is confirmed semantically because .secrets is intentionally loaded.

dangerous_patterns.title: npx Execution of Alpha Dependency
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill runs an alpha package through npx, which can execute downloaded code from the package registry.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is direct remote package execution. The package may be legitimate, but the supply-chain risk is real.

dangerous_patterns.title: Direct Push to Main in Fix Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The fix validation example commits and pushes directly to main. This can bypass branch review controls if copied without adapting the workflow.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The repository mutation is explicit. The risk is procedural rather than malicious, so confidence is moderate-high.

content.value_statement: Manual GitHub Actions setup can create slow, fragile, or over-permissive pipelines. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through CI/CD design, optimization, security checks, and debugging.
actual_capabilities: Plans GitHub Actions pipeline stages for build, test, security scan, package, and deployment flows.
Suggests matrix testing strategies across operating systems and runtime versions.
Provides workflow optimization patterns for caching, path filters, parallel jobs, and Docker layer caching.
Guides workflow security reviews for token permissions, action pinning, secrets handling, and scanning tools.
Outlines debugging steps for failed workflow runs, local replay, retry logic, and timeout fixes.
Coordinates specialized AI roles for CI/CD design, testing, security, and performance analysis.
limitations: It does not include the referenced helper scripts or workflow template files.
It cannot verify repository-specific secrets, branch protections, or deployment environments by itself.
Some examples require local tools such as act, git, npx, and GitHub API helpers.
Security-sensitive debug examples must be edited before use in real CI logs.

use_cases.title: Create a New CI Pipeline
use_cases.description: Design a GitHub Actions workflow with build, lint, test, security scan, and manual dispatch stages.

use_cases.title: Reduce Slow Workflow Runs
use_cases.description: Analyze bottlenecks and apply caching, path filtering, and job parallelization to shorten CI feedback time.

use_cases.title: Harden Existing Automation
use_cases.description: Review token scopes, action versions, secrets handling, and scanning coverage before expanding deployment workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a Basic CI Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a repository without GitHub Actions.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a GitHub Actions CI workflow for my project. First inspect the project type, package manager, test command, and build command. Then propose the workflow stages and explain any assumptions before creating files.

prompt_templates.title: Add Matrix Testing
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate workflow expansion across runtimes or operating systems.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to add matrix testing to my existing GitHub Actions workflow. Cover supported operating systems and runtime versions, keep failures readable, and recommend exclusions for unsupported combinations.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize CI Performance
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my GitHub Actions workflow for speed. Identify slow jobs, cache opportunities, redundant steps, and jobs that can run in parallel. Provide a prioritized change list with risk notes.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Deployment Pipeline
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced security and release readiness review.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to audit my GitHub Actions deployment workflow. Check token permissions, action pinning, secrets handling, branch protections, deployment approvals, rollback steps, and debug logging risks.

output_examples.input: Create CI for a Node.js package with tests and linting.
output: A staged workflow plan covering install, lint, test, build, and security scan jobs.
Recommended cache keys for dependency installation.
A short list of repository settings to confirm before enabling deployment.

output_examples.input: My workflow is slow and often reruns unnecessary jobs.
output: A bottleneck summary grouped by dependency installation, test execution, and Docker build time.
Suggested path filters and parallel job changes.
A validation plan for comparing before and after run duration.

output_examples.input: Review my deployment workflow before production use.
output: A security checklist for token scope, action pinning, protected environments, and secrets exposure.
Warnings for debug steps that may print sensitive values.
A release-readiness summary with required fixes and optional improvements.

best_practices: Review every generated workflow before committing, especially permissions, secrets, and deployment steps.
Pin third-party actions to trusted versions or commit SHAs and document update procedures.
Remove environment dumps, verbose secret handling, and temporary debug steps before merging.
anti_patterns: Do not copy debug steps that print all environment variables into shared CI logs.
Do not push workflow fixes directly to main when branch review is required.
Do not run remote package commands or helper scripts without checking their source and version.

faq.question: Does this skill create complete GitHub Actions workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides structured guidance for workflow stages, testing, security checks, deployment, and optimization.

faq.question: Can it migrate from Jenkins, Travis, or CircleCI?
faq.answer: It can guide migration planning, but repository-specific commands and deployment details still need human review.

faq.question: Does it include ready-to-run helper scripts?
faq.answer: No. The skill references helper scripts and templates, but the analyzed package only includes documentation and a diagram.

faq.question: Is it safe to use with repository secrets?
faq.answer: Use caution. Remove debug logging that prints environment variables, and avoid verbose secret-file replay in shared logs.

faq.question: Which AI tools does it support?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk level high?
faq.answer: The skill is not confirmed malicious, but it includes command execution, remote package execution, and risky secret debugging patterns.
